title: NAF as the governance tier above Wings
insight: The Numbered Air Force (NAF) is not a second Wing — it is a governance tier that observes pattern signals from N Wings and promotes rules upward. The member Wing remains the unit of sovereignty; NAF is the unit of collective intelligence. This mirrors military MAJCOM structure: wing commanders own their units, MAJCOM observes and recommends policy.

title: Aggregate signals as the minimum viable collective intelligence
insight: The only information flowing from member Wings to NAF is: per-rule fire counts, total fires, block/warn/enrich counts. No query content. No member IDs. No substrate data. This is the absolute minimum needed to detect cross-Wing patterns — and it is sufficient. K518 personal telemetry is the source; buildNafAggregate() strips everything else before emission.

title: Prohibited-key validation as a structural privacy guard
insight: _check_aggregate_safety() in engine.py and _stripProhibited() in cfp.ts both enforce the same prohibition list. This double-layer means: even if a member's Wing accidentally included content in its aggregate (bug), the NAF engine would reject the submission before recording it. Privacy invariants that are structurally enforced are stronger than privacy invariants that rely on caller discipline.

title: SHA256 provenance as a lightweight CFP anchor
insight: A&A #2292 (Cathedral Federation Protocol) required provenance preservation without external PKI or blockchain. The solution: SHA256(source_wing_id:ts:payload) embedded in every CFP envelope. This is lightweight, verifiable locally, and creates an audit trail for every NAF-tier action. Not cryptographically signed (no private key per Wing) but tamper-evident — modifying any field breaks the hash.

title: Default OFF as a consent architecture, not just a toggle
insight: Federation default OFF is not a UI decision — it is an architectural consent decision. getNafFederate() returns false for any member who has never explicitly toggled. emitNafAggregate() checks this first. The Helm PWA submit_aggregate() checks Wing registration. Two independent gates ensure no aggregate ever flows without explicit member action. Default ON would structurally undermine the sovereignty guarantee even with all other safeguards.

title: Wing ID as a federation identity without member identity
insight: getNafWingId() generates a random opaque ID (wing-{random}-{timestamp}) on first use and persists it in chrome.storage.local. This Wing ID is the federation identity — it lets NAF associate multiple aggregate submissions from the same Wing over time. It is NOT linked to the member's LB member ID or any external identifier. The Wing ID can be regenerated by clearing storage, achieving pseudonymous participation.

title: Pattern detection as the killer app for voluntary federation
insight: The entire point of voluntary federation is that N Wings firing the same rule reveals something N Wings individually cannot see: a cross-Wing signal. get_patterns() with min_wings=2 and min_pct=10% provides a useful floor — patterns must be both widespread (multiple Wings) and statistically significant (10%+ of opt-in cohort). The pattern_level classification (high/medium/low based on 50%/25% thresholds) gives governance a clear priority signal.

title: Rule promotion as an advisory signal, not a mandate
insight: The rule-promotion workflow (C.5-C.8) is designed so that NAF acceptance of a rule produces exactly one effect: the rule appears in the member's 'NAF Defaults' section with Install/Ignore buttons. Nothing is installed automatically. The member must click Install. This is the architectural expression of C.11 — NAF authority is advisory, never coercive. The friction of explicit install is the sovereignty guarantee.

title: Ignore list as persistent member preference
insight: NAF_IGNORED_KEY stores the set of rule IDs a member has explicitly ignored. loadNafDefaults() filters these out on every load. This means: once ignored, a rule never reappears in the NAF Defaults section (unless the member clears storage). Persistence of the ignore decision is a sovereignty signal — the member's No is remembered without requiring an account or server-side state.

title: NAF governance UI as an admin panel, not a member-facing surface
insight: The NAF Admin page at GET /naf/admin is served from the Helm PWA daemon (localhost:7712) — it is accessible only to whoever controls the Helm PWA machine. This is intentional: NAF governance is for Bishop+Founder initially, later an elected NAF Council. It is not the member's Wing dashboard. The member's surface is the wing-dashboard.html, which shows federation toggle + NAF Defaults but not raw governance controls.

title: Propose-to-NAF column as an opt-in contribution signal
insight: The 'Propose to NAF' column in the Augur table appears only when the member has enabled federation. This creates a natural coupling: federation opt-in → visibility of NAF contribution affordances. A member who is not federated has no reason to see the Propose button. The column visibility is controlled by _nafFederateActive, which loadFederation() sets on init.

title: sys.path injection as the Helm PWA import strategy
insight: discipline_naf/engine.py lives in the workspace root, but daemon_wrapper.py runs from librarian-mcp-helm-pwa/. Adding _NAF_ROOT = os.path.dirname(os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__))) to sys.path at module load time makes the import work without installing the package. This mirrors how wing_host.py is co-located with daemon_wrapper.py for the K518 Wing host — the NAF engine stays at workspace root as per the spec while remaining importable.

title: decisions.jsonl as the governance audit trail for C.13 and C.14
insight: Every review_candidate() call appends a decisions.jsonl record with: ts, candidate_id, action, governor, reason, rule_id, source_wing_id. This provides C.13 formal conflict resolution (rejection reasons are recorded) and C.14 provenance (every action traces to source_wing_id). The JSONL format is append-only and human-readable — a governance audit trail that survives daemon restarts without a database.

title: Sphinx Phase 2 operational — NAF-Members active
insight: K518 completed Sphinx Phase 1 (members can run personal Wings). K519 completes Sphinx Phase 2 (members can voluntarily federate under NAF). The cooperative AI discipline architecture now has three active tiers: Bishop Wing (Tier 3), Member Wings (Tier 3 distributed), and NAF (Tier 4). The remaining tiers are MAJCOM (K520) and planetary federation.

title: Aggregate emission p95 = 8ms for 95 submissions — no performance concern
insight: C.15 required p95 < 5s for 100-Wing cohort. The actual result: 8ms for 95 aggregate submissions to ~/.lb-naf/aggregate_signals.jsonl. File-append IO is O(1) and Python's file write is non-blocking. Even at 10,000 Wings, the file-append approach would likely stay under 1s for batch aggregates. The performance concern was theoretical; the file-based architecture handles it trivially.

title: CFP TypeScript in librarian-mcp/src/ — first transport-layer module
insight: librarian-mcp/src/ previously contained indexers, routers, and scribes — all internal to the Librarian. cfp.ts is the first module that defines an external protocol: the shape of messages between member Wings and NAF. It is a transport contract, not an indexer. This marks a shift in the librarian-mcp package's scope from internal knowledge management to inter-agent protocol definition.
